outcome_contract.why: The prime guest app shows activity finish times based on a fixed 2-hour assumption. Staff-run activities vary in length. Without a configurable duration field, every activity incorrectly shows the same finish time, and the live/ended lifecycle is wrong for any activity that isn't exactly 2 hours.
outcome_contract.intended_outcome_statement: Activity finish times and lifecycle states reflect the real planned duration stored per instance, defaulting to 120 minutes when no duration is set.

engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Plan must update `formatFinishTime` signature and one call site

engineering_coverage.applicable: Both `resolveLifecycle` copies updated → correct live/ended boundary
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Plan must update both copies atomically; test coverage for non-default duration

engineering_coverage.applicable: Existing test passes; add non-default duration test case
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Add one test fixture with `durationMinutes: 30` to confirm lifecycle boundary

engineering_coverage.applicable: `ActivityInstance` type gains optional field; RTDB schemaless
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Adds new RTDB path, new type shape
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: RTDB records without field safe; old instances behave identically to today

engineering_coverage.applicable: Trivial arithmetic; no new network call
Additive type change; `git revert` rolls back fully
engineering_coverage.current_or_option_state: Rollback also requires RTDB path cleanup
engineering_coverage.gap_or_implication: Clean rollback: revert 4 files; no DB state

stage_payload.decision_summary: Activity finish times and lifecycle states in the prime guest app are calculated using a hardcoded 2-hour (120-minute) constant. This constant appears in three places across two files. The decision is: how should duration become data-driven? The answer is unambiguous from the evidence — add an optional `durationMinutes` field to `ActivityInstance` and replace the constant with `Math.max(1, activity.durationMinutes ?? 120)`. The only non-trivial sub-decision is whether to guard against invalid zero/negative values, which analysis resolves here.
goals: Activity finish times reflect the actual planned duration of each instance
Live/ended lifecycle state is correct for non-120-minute activities
Backwards-compatible: existing instances without `durationMinutes` behave identically to today (120-minute default)
All three hardcoded call sites updated atomically
non_goals: Staff activity creation UI within prime — no such UI exists; out of scope
Firebase security rules changes — duration is display metadata, no auth implications
constraints: RTDB is schemaless (NoSQL JSON tree) — no migration, but no enforcement either; `durationMinutes` will be absent on all existing instances
No Zod schema validates `ActivityInstance` at runtime — TypeScript-only type update sufficient
`chat/channel/page.tsx` has a copy-paste of `resolveLifecycle` — must be updated in the same change
assumptions: Staff will set `durationMinutes` via Firebase console when creating new instances; existing instances fall back to 120 min
`durationMinutes` values are positive integers (minutes); zero or negative should be guarded against

evaluation_criteria.Criterion: Correct finish time for non-120-min activities
evaluation_criteria.Why it matters: Core goal — current state is broken for any duration ≠ 2h
Existing RTDB instances must not break

evaluation_criteria.Criterion: Zero code changes outside the 4 affected files

options_considered.Option: A — Add `durationMinutes?: number` to type + update 3 call sites
options_considered.Description: Optional field on `ActivityInstance`; `Math.max(1, durationMinutes ?? 120)` in both `resolveLifecycle` and `formatFinishTime`
options_considered.Upside: Zero migration; backwards compatible; minimal surface area; 4 files only
options_considered.Downside: Staff must set field via Firebase console (no in-app creation UI)

options_considered.Description: Store duration in a separate path, e.g. `messaging/activities/config/{instanceId}/durationMinutes`
options_considered.Upside: Clean separation of display config from instance data
options_considered.Downside: Requires a second Firebase read per activity; no existing pattern; over-engineered for a single field

options_considered.Option: C — Remove finish time display entirely
options_considered.Description: Stop showing end time; only show start time
options_considered.Key risks: Degrades UX; guests lose ability to plan

chosen_approach.Recommendation: Option A — add `durationMinutes?: number` to `ActivityInstance`, update `formatFinishTime` to accept the full `ActivityInstance` object, and update both `resolveLifecycle` copies to use `Math.max(1, activity.durationMinutes ?? 120)`.
chosen_approach.Why this wins: Option A is the minimal, correct fix. The type system already describes `ActivityInstance` exhaustively — adding one optional field follows the established pattern. No new network calls, no new RTDB paths, no migration. Option B introduces an unnecessary fan-in pattern for a single field. Option C degrades UX with no benefit.
chosen_approach.What it depends on: Firebase RTDB schemaless behavior (confirmed); no Zod schema on `ActivityInstance` (confirmed); `formatFinishTime` has a single call site (confirmed at `ActivitiesClient.tsx:139`).

rejected_approaches: **Option B (separate RTDB config node)** — over-engineered for a single optional field. Creates a two-source fan-in for lifecycle resolution with no compensating benefit. Rejected.
**Option C (remove finish time)** — removes useful guest information to avoid fixing the root cause. Rejected.

sequencing_constraints: Type update must precede UI call-site changes (TypeScript compiler enforces)
Both `resolveLifecycle` copies should be updated in the same commit for atomicity
Tests come last (validate the completed implementation)
risks_to_carry_forward: The two `resolveLifecycle` functions in different files are identical today — no divergence risk for this fix, but the duplication itself is a maintenance liability. Plan should note the duplication as a known code smell (out of scope to refactor now).

process_topology.note: None: no material process topology change. The change modifies three utility functions and one TypeScript interface within the prime guest app. No CI/deploy lane, operator runbook, Firebase security rule, or multi-step workflow is altered.
